Rewritten it into the following, to avoid possibilities of using one shared index object:
```python
self.df1.index = np.arange(...)
self.df2.index = np.arange(...)
```

Probably this is an excessive safety because since the input object for the index are numpy arrays, we are forced to convert them to pandas Indices and so create a new object every time we assign a new index:
```python
>>> common_index_np = np.arange(1, len(df1) + 1)
>>> df1.index = common_index_np
>>> df2.index = common_index_np
>>> df1.index is df2.index
False
```
But if someday somebody would change a numpy array to pandas Index, then we will get a shared index objects, which we don't want to:
```python
>>> common_index_pandas = pandas.RangeIndex(1, len(df1) + 1)
>>> df1.index = common_index_pandas
>>> df2.index = common_index_pandas
>>> df1.index is df2.index
True
```
So I just create a new index object for every frame with the appropriate in-code comment to avoid this.

The risk with this approach is that the `real name` is correctly resolved only if that user has appeared in one of the documents processed during the enrichment phase. Thus, depending on the enrichment phase (and the documents processed in it), the real name can be set with the right info or with `Unknown`.

A solution is to initialize the dict `username_mapping` with the usernames and real names present in the enriched index, thus  `username_mapping` doesn't depend on the information of the single enrichment phase. Another solution is to implement a study, which is executed after the enrichment phase. However, to avoid adding too much complexity in this iteration, it may be convenient to show only the `username`. The real name resolution can be done afterwards.

Even if we wanted to support both comma-separated and tokenized lists,
it would be easy to accomplish with a simple custom action:

```python
import argparse


class ExtendFromAction(argparse.Action):
    def __call__(self, parser, namespace, values, option_string=None):
        items = getattr(namespace, self.dest, None) or []
        items.extend(x for xs in values for x in xs)
        setattr(namespace, self.dest, items)


parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
parser.add_argument(
    "--plugins", nargs="*", action=ExtendFromAction, type=lambda x: x.split(",")
)

print(parser.parse_args(["--plugins", "a,b", "c,d", "--plugins", "e,f"]))
# prints: Namespace(plugins=['a', 'b', 'c', 'd', 'e', 'f'])
```

Or, we could just use `nargs="*", type=lambda x: x.split(",")` and
flatten with a helper function in the application code.

The `fix_flags` used by the plugin system is mainly used to signal
errors due to _interactions_ of flags: “if inspect-mode is set but not
exactly one of a logdir or event file is given, then die”. As long as
all we’re doing is controlling the parsing of a _single_ flag, I’d
prefer to do so with `type=` or `action=` or similar rather than adding
a new mechanism.

I was unaware the existing `log` endpoint was designed to handle rich events. But even then, I'd prefer to have a new endpoint that accepts a new, more specific type. This would just make it easier on the service-side to parse and ingest the engine event, since we could walk the specific event if we need to do something more complicated. (e.g. put it into different tables, update some index manually, etc.)

While the "union type" isn't great, it does provide an exact type definition for every event that we can accept.
```
type EngineEvent struct {
	CancelEvent      *CancelEvent       `json:"cancelEvent,omitempty"`
	StdoutEvent      *StdoutEngineEvent `json:"stdoutEvent,omitempty"`
	DiagnosticEvent  *DiagnosticEvent   `json:"diagnosticEvent,omitempty"`
	PreludeEvent     *PreludeEvent      `json:"preludeEvent,omitempty"`
	SummaryEvent     *SummaryEvent      `json:"summaryEvent,omitempty"`
	ResourcePreEvent *ResourcePreEvent  `json:"resourcePreEvent,omitempty"`
	ResOutputsEvent  *ResOutputsEvent   `json:"resOutputsEvent,omitempty"`
	ResOpFailedEvent *ResOpFailedEvent  `json:"resOpFailedEvent,omitempty"`
}
```

Whereas using the existing log handler, would need us to convert the `Fields` map to whatever specific type associated with the engine event, if we need it somewhere in the service API.

```
type AppendUpdateLogEntryRequest struct {
	Kind   string                 `json:"kind"`
	Fields map[string]interface{} `json:"fields"`
}
```

Is there some advantage to the logs endpoint I'm missing? Other than it can accept new engine events without requiring bumping the service dependency of pulumi/pulumi?

I think this would be a rather small addition, but I'd prefer to do it in a separate PR in order to avoid growing this one larger.

We would like to enable this in future, but I intentionally left it out for now from this PR (to reduce its complexity)
- The probing on 15.4 is much less useful, since if the neighbor link supports both TREL/WiFi and 15.4, we would in general prefer TREL for frame tx.
- We have the receive mechanism to discover the radio linkes (e.g. if/when a 15.4 link becomes available). For a router neighbor MLE Link Adv would be send on all radio links and potentially received by neighbors on multiple radio links as well). 
- Probing is mainly intended for cases where we are actively sending a lot of data/frames and want to quickly discover if a better radio link (TREL/WIFi in this case) has become available to switch to it quickly.

There are some other points to consider for probing on 15.4 links:
- When probing on 15.4 we don't want to use the same max tx retries (e.g., we use 15 tx attempts for direct tx). 
- Otherwise if the 15.4 link is not available, we would spend/waste a lot of time retx-ing the frame 15 times over the 15.4 link and waiting for ack timeout.
- So for this, we need to use mechanism to set a different tx retry count for probe frame vs normal data per radio type (we have this behavior in sub-MAC layer but need to make sure the radio platform also supports this to cover the cases where radio platform layer is handling the frame retx logic).

### Contact information

The [policy][1] isn't very specific about what format `<contact information>` should follow:
```
<client name>/<version> (<contact information>) <library/framework name>/<version> [<library name>/<version> ...]
```
and they just provide this example:
```
User-Agent: CoolBot/0.0 (https://example.org/coolbot/; coolbot@example.org) generic-library/0.0
```
but sure, will remove the email address.

### `urllib.request`'s default `User-Agent`

The default header (`[('User-agent', 'Python-urllib/3.9')]`) gets rate-limited, as mentioned in #43.

The [policy][1] specifically asks not to use this:

> _Do not use generic agents such as "curl", "lwp", "Python-urllib", and so on._

and specifically asks to have the keyword "bot" provided:

> _If you run an automated agent, please consider following the Internet-wide convention of including the string "bot" in the User-Agent string, in any combination of lowercase or uppercase letters. This is recognized by Wikimedia's systems, and used to classify traffic and provide more accurate statistics._

[1]: https://meta.wikimedia.org/wiki/User-Agent_policy

@StrikerRUS , thank you for doing this exploration.

I'm not opposed to this change, and I've committed the suggested patch, but I think there are some considerations the maintainers should be aware of.  

One of the challenges in this patch was coaxing three different build systems (Boost's B2, cmake, and MSVC/make) to cooperate.  And as you know I was originally targeting more than just the Windows platform.  I found that the Boost dependency list differed significantly by platform -- on Linux (generally) only the few expected dependent submodules were built, on Windows (generally), many more submodules were fetched.   This was a bit surprising, but I didn't spend a lot of time chasing down why, because it seemed to me that downloading all submodules was always safe, and that even if I figured it all out for each platform, it would lead to complicated platform checking code that might turn out to be fragile in the long run (meaning a risk of not working anymore if the Boost libs or any of the three build systems changed or started interacting differently).

Of course I realize that there may be costs associated with fetching and processing code unecessarily in the CI pipeline, and also that non-Windows builds may never be needed, so I'm OK with the suggested change.  I just wanted to alert you all that you may have to rethink this targeted download approach if other platforms are added or the build becomes unstable.

```python

def iterate_service_operations() -> Generator[Tuple[ServiceModel, OperationModel], None, None]:
    """
    Returns one record per operation in the AWS service spec, where the first item is the service model the operation
    belongs to, and the second is the operation model.

    :return: an iterable
    """
    for service in list_services():
        for op_name in service.operation_names:
            yield service, service.operation_model(op_name)

We can use a `_known_plugins` class attribute if we decide that we don't want anything to inherit from `imopen`. Especially for complex types like `list` or `dict` this can lead to hard to find bugs and unexpected behavior because we are now sharing state between instances of parent classes and child classes.

```
class A(object):
    _known_plugins = ["A", "B", "C"]

class B(A):
    def __init__(self):
        self._known_plugins.append("D")

b = B()
a = A()
print(f"A knows {a._known_plugins}", f"B knows {b._known_plugins}")
#  prints: A knows ['A', 'B', 'C', 'D'] B knows ['A', 'B', 'C', 'D']
```

This means that `b` is now sharing state with `a`. I don't know if this is a good idea or not. You run the risk of downstream code relying on `a` having a "D" plugin, which suddenly disappears when code in a sister module gets refactored for example like

```
class A(object):
    _known_plugins = ["A", "B", "C"]

class B(A):
    def __init__(self):
        # only use D to avoid conflict with 
        # latest features of plugin A
        self._known_plugins = ["D"]

b = B()
a = A()
print(f"A knows {a._known_plugins}", f"B knows {b._known_plugins}")
# prints: A knows ['A', 'B', 'C'] B knows ['D']
```

Now all code that relied on instances of `A` knowing plugin D will break with a cryptic error message like `"No plugin to load images of type XYZ"`, which doesn't indicate at all that changes to `B` are the cause. Also, `A` and `B` no longer share state, but a new class `C` will still share state with `A` when it naively inherits from `B`.

```
class C(B):
    def __init__(self):
        # plugins are shared with parent class
        self._known_plugins.append("E")

print(C()._known_plugins)
# prints ['A', 'B', 'C', 'E']
```

but 

```
class C(B):
    def __init__(self):
        # maybe not so shared with parent class
        super(C, self).__init__()
        self._known_plugins.append("E")

print(f"C knows {C()._known_plugins}", f"B knows {B()._known_plugins}")
# prints: C knows ['D', 'E'] B knows ['D']
```

I'd rather have it clean and at all times say "if you want to use parent's plugins, call the parent's constructor via `super`".

---

We will still have to use `_imopen` trickery. This comes from `imopen` being a factory, not from it being a singleton. `imopen()` will still be a constructor call to `imopen.__init__` and return an instance of `imopen`, where we actually want it to call `imopen.__call__` and return an instance of a plugin class. Maybe I'm missing something though x)

---

I think it is cleaner to have `__enter__` and `__exit__` methods defined in the plugin. Not every plugin will want to keep a file open for a prolonged period of time and some might want to keep multiple files open, e.g., if you implement a `DatasetFeeder` plugin that takes a `CSV` file with locations of images, stacks them up into batches and feeds them to the user, perhaps including some repetition.

The latter is a more esoteric example (will become more relevant once we move towards reading video though), but the former is true for all legacy plugins.

This is correct, but you could make it even more clean by parametrizing with a list of files instead of passing `file1` and `file2`. For example:
```
$ cat test_parametrize.py
import pytest

@pytest.mark.parametrize("arg", ["arg1", "arg2", ["arg3.a", "arg3.b"]])
def test_list(arg):
     assert arg is not None
$
```

In the example above the test is run 3 times. First with `"arg1"`, then `"arg2"`, and finally with the list `["arg3.a", "arg3.b"]`

This way your test can be expanded on in the future without changing its signature every time, and it becomes easier to reason about

```python
def partition_get_data_label(partition_data,
                             allow_tuple=True,
                             allow_list=True,
                             has_label=True):
    """
    :param partition_data: The data partition from Spark RDD, which should be a list of records.
    :param allow_tuple: Boolean. Whether the model accepts a tuple as input. Default is True.
    :param allow_list: Boolean. Whether the model accepts a list as input. Default is True.
    :param has_label: Boolean. Whether the data partition contains labels.
    :return: Concatenated data for the data partition.
    """
    data_list = [data['x'] for data in partition_data]
    label_list = [data['y'] for data in partition_data]

    data = _convert_list_tuple(combine(data_list),
                               allow_tuple=allow_tuple, allow_list=allow_list)
    if has_label:
        label = _convert_list_tuple(combine(label_list),
                                    allow_tuple=allow_tuple, allow_list=allow_list)
    else:
        label = None

    return data, label
```

I found that the following test used the old behavior and printed as expected on 3.7 but 2.7 still gave the help text back.

```
import sys
sys.path.append('../python-fire')
import fire


class Value(object):
    """Original question"""

    def __init__(self, name):
        self.name = name

    def __repr__(self):
        return "%s(name=%r)" % (self.__class__.__name__, self.name)


class NewValue:
    """As expressed in comments"""

    def __init__(self, name):
        self.name = name

    def __repr__(self):
        return "%s(name=%r)" % (self.__class__.__name__, self.name)

    def __str__(self):
        return repr(self)

# Should print help
fire.Fire({'key1': Value('value1'), 'key2': Value('value2')})
# Should print SimpleGroup
fire.Fire({'key1': NewValue('value1'), 'key2': NewValue('value2')})
```

At this point I had implemented the modification to `IsValue` in `value_types`:

```
def IsValue(component):
  return isinstance(component, VALUE_TYPES) or (
      hasattr(component, '__str__') and inspect.ismethod(component.__str__))
```


I went to verify the criteria for a SimpleGroup as you outlined in the recommended approach; `core.py` -> `value_types.py` -> `IsValue(component)`. The criteria is that the `result` is a dictionary and that `value_types.IsSimpleGroup(result)` return a truthy value: (I know you know, just brain dumping what I was looking at.)

https://github.com/google/python-fire/blob/04e2434001e809bdf05e6f47d71452d1e8614b78/fire/core.py#L261-L262

The value is obviously a dictionary so I checked the return of `value_types.IsSimpleGroup(result)` and found that it was returning `True` and I was dropping into line 262 attempting to print the dictionary as string but... it was returning an empty dictionary; but only on Python 2.7.

So I took a look at `_DictAsString`, specifically at this section:

https://github.com/google/python-fire/blob/04e2434001e809bdf05e6f47d71452d1e8614b78/fire/core.py#L315-L323

After a bit of debug I found that the `MemberVisible` test was returning `False`. This seemed odd considering there was different behavior on Python 3.7 so I kept going.

I took a look at `completion.MemberVisible` and iterated through the conditional list to find which was was returning `False` and found it was the following statement:

https://github.com/google/python-fire/blob/04e2434001e809bdf05e6f47d71452d1e8614b78/fire/completion.py#L321-L322

Knowing that the test results were different between 2.7 and 3.7, I popped the statements in the repl to see the outputs.

```
Python 2.7.16 (default, Dec 13 2019, 18:00:32) 
[GCC 4.2.1 Compatible Apple LLVM 11.0.0 (clang-1100.0.32.4) (-macos10.15-objc-s on darwin
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> from __future__ import absolute_import
>>> type(absolute_import)
<type 'instance'>
```

vs

```
Python 3.7.6 (default, Dec 30 2019, 19:38:26)
[Clang 11.0.0 (clang-1100.0.33.16)] on darwin
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> from __future__ import absolute_import
>>> type(absolute_import)
<class '__future__._Feature'>
```

I have not discovered exactly why the 2.7 `__future__.absolute_import` returns an instance and 3.5+ `__future__.absolute_import` returns a class. It seems there may be a difference in `import` between 2.7 and 3 where 2.7 returns an instance of the module and 3.7 returns the actual class reference.

The other possiblity is that I've missed something obvious and it's none of the above :).

Either way, the functionality of this if statement seems to be to hide instances of `__future__` probably to keep the noise down. Given what I understand of the import behavior between 2.7 and 3.4+, this result will only be achieved if the version of python is 3.4+ as currently implemented. The `six` module provides the builtin to test the version of python and I see `six.PY27` is used elsewhere so I chose to lock this conditional to 3.4+.

I'm sure there are other approaches. This approach seemed to require the fewest changes to the code while retaining functionality. It is quite possible that I've missed the intent of the if or some other functionality it provides.

AssetObservations should land soon, and then you'll be able to write a job that logs arbitrary metadata about any asset. from dagster import asset, DataFrameDagsterType, job, create_monitor_asset_op

@asset(dagster_type=DataFrameDagsterType(column_constraints...))

    ...

monitor_asset1 = create_monitor_asset_op(asset1)

@job
def my_job():
    monitor_asset1()
```

The new check is added in `HandleFrameRequest()`.
- `HandleFrameRequest()` is called when the Mac layer is ready to transmit a frame and asks the next layer (`MeshForwarder`) to prepare the frame to be sent.
- At this point, the frame has not yet been sent and the `TxSuccess` status has not yet been updated or set.
- If we want to do such a check, we should probably move it to the `HandleFrameSend()` call path and after the `mSendMessage->SetTxSuccess(false)` call in `MeshForwarder::UpdateSendMessage()`.

I removed the whitespace_split=True here due to the following reason: there are two types of chars in command: punctuation chars and whitespace. Punctuation chars should be parsed in the first place and then whitespace. Based on [shlex.whitespace_split](https://docs.python.org/3.4/library/shlex.html#shlex.shlex.whitespace_split), when it sets to True, the command string will just be split by whitespace but not punctuation chars, which can be error-prone.

it's better that you don't raise SystemExit inside here or similar main-like functions that are used as console scripts entry points. Just return the exit code `return 1`, and the wrapper will raise `sys.exit(your_entry_point())` for you. Otherwise when you call stemhist in the test suite, and it fails, it will stop the whole test run and pytest process will exit abruptly (see `test_cli.py::test_stemhist` where you call `stemhist` function without catching SystemExit)

I checked out how what you described can be implemented.
First, let me try to make you visualize the execution flow:
```
Transport -> on_message -> handle_message_* -> RaidenService::handle_state_change -> WAL -> node::state_transition -> node::(handle_processed | handle_delivered)
```
This is the call flow. Which means that if i wanted to make on_message return `True` based on whether the processed / delivered messages were processed correctly by the node.py handler, i would have to update all methods `handle_mesage_*` in `message_handler.py` to return `True/False` based on the result. That being said, `RaidenService::handle_state_change` returns the resulting events list from the state change which means that i will either have to introduce an event type just to indicate the result of `Processed` or `Delivered` processing for the `handle_message_*` to return `True/False`. 
I hope the above makes sense to you, otherwise, please follow the call flow and think about what i am seeing. TBH, I am not sure this change would be worth the effort.

1) Subscribe all actors, don't establish connections immediately but only save actor info in a local map. Establish connections on demand when submitting actor tasks.
2) Don't subscribe anything initially. When receiving a new actor task,  look up actor info in gcs and establish a connection. And then also subscribe that particular actor.

This can be avoided for this filter. You have the metadata of the spatial domain image (the input), and you are returning spatial domain images as well, we can restore the metadata. Set origin, spacing and direction of the outputs depending on the level.

In python:
```python
def restoreMetadata(imageToRestore, referenceImage, level):
    spacing = referenceImage.GetSpacing()
    origin = referenceImage.GetOrigin()
    direction = referenceImage.GetDirection()
    imageToRestore.SetSpacing(spacing * (2**level) )
    imageToRestore.SetOrigin(origin)
    imageToRestore.SetDirection(direction)
    return imageToRestore
```
And remember that the N-1 output (low pass residual) corresponds to the `l` level.

This is not required. It is a general purpose method to add pre-built objects to the linker args in the elaboration command. I can be used to wrap GHDL in a C program, to provide the implementation of VHPI functions/procedures or for both features at the same time. In this example, both features are used. But that's not a requirement.

Moreover, the provided VHPI functions can be any other defined by the user in the testbench or in custom packages. This is because VHPI functions declared in multiple different VHDL sources/modules can then be provided all together in a single fat object or in multiple slimmer objects.

Nonetheless, I agree that the name could be better. But I think that the name is tightly related to how does the user tell that VHPI is to be used. E.g., the user might want to use VHPI for custom functions, but not have the 'external' vectors/memories. In this context, the objects added through `set_objects` are not required to define neither `write_byte` nor `read_byte`. Precisely, `extmem_pkg-novhpi` should be added to the project.

1. Add an `__init__` method that checks if `contact` was included in the given arguments, setting a private (prefixed with an underscore) boolean variable with the result. See `mixins.py` about how to set this attribute.

So on startup, we try to get the current version's DB from the `settings` table. If you just ran a new Raiden version, say version 17 while you were on 16 before that, the DB file will be empty... no tables will exist there. Therefore, we try to get the version (if the database is valid)... if it's not (no tables exist on a brand new file) then an `OperationalError` will be thrown, by that time we know that we have a new file that hasn't been populated with tables yet so we return 0.

Why would you want to do the estimate gas before the preconditions checks? What does this achieve?

>  the question is can there be race conditions with the above strategy?

Yes, there will always be races, the preconditions checks are only best effort to avoid sending an unnecessary transactions, but races cannot prevented. And as you point out the races are handled properly, this is because of work for #1966.

> For open this should hold. No new block should invalidate preconditions. If a new block comes and someone else opened the channel that's already handled.

Using this as an example. The precondition "there is not channel among A-B before sending the transaction" can be invalidated by these events:

- User A and B wants to open channel at the same time
- Both run the precondition checks
- The transaction of one of the nodes will fail

The above shows when the precondition will be invalidated, the check prior to sending the transaction is to avoid an unnecessary transaction which wastes gas, the check after the transaction failed is to make debugging easier.

The problem is *not* about the preconditions being invalidated. The problem is having a **consistent** view of the blockchain.

I think your solution works, however it's confusing to me. We need to use a single block to make sure the data we retrieve for a given channel is consistent, for this reason I would stick to for all things in a proxy.

Note regarding the estimate gas call: I realized that it doesn't make sense to use the same block as the one for the checks, we should use latest here, so I would do this latest as to have the freshest view of the blockchain, perhaps this should be the pattern:

```python
preconditions()
gas = gas_estimate()
if gas is not None:
  txhash = send_transaction()
  result = poll(txhash)
if gas is None or result is Failure:
  detect_failure()
```

the import detector will flag imports as weak if they won't obviously fail module loading. For example:
```python
import mypackage
```
will fail the module load if "mypackage" isn't importable, but 
```python
try:
    import mypackage
    has_mypackage = True
except ModuleNotFoundError:
    has_mypackage = False
```
is a pattern for optionally using "mypackage". Since it is valid for "mypackage" to not be inferable, the detector marks it as a weak import. If we don't resolve the import here, we flag it as `weak_ignore` so that Pants can surface to a user that it has detected this import, has not found the thing it imports, and is ok with that. (This was the original motivation for me adding the debug feature.)

Hi @kenodegard,

I spent a good amount of time deliberating your suggestion and going back over a lot of the code contained within `conda/common/configuration.py` and `conda/base/context.py`.

What I concluded is that although it's alluring to overload the `element_type` parameter, this parameter really only wants to point at a single type. Let me explain what I mean with an example...

Suppose we have the following configuration defined:

```python
from conda.common.configuration import (
    Configuration,
    SequenceParameter,
    PrimitiveParameter,
   MapParameter,
    ParameterLoader, 

class MyConfig(Configuration):
    complex_seq = ParameterLoader(
        SequenceParameter(element_type=(
            PrimitiveParameter(0, element_type=int),
            PrimitiveParameter("", element_type=str),
            MapParameter(PrimitiveParameter("", element_type=str))
        ))
    )
```

We've added in `PrimitiveParameter` twice, which may cause undefined behavior. When we run across a number, do we parse it as a string or an integer? Defining `element_type` as a sequence of `Parameter` types leaves us open to this type of ambiguity in our API.

But, what if we just want to enforce that users only pass in one of each type. In this case, we would have to implement some ugly validation logic within these objects to verify that. There must be a better way...

This is why I opted to create two new keyword arguments for this `Parameter` type. To avoid ambiguities like in the above example, we want to establish a one-to-one relation between what is found in the configuration and how it ultimately ends up getting parsed.

I would gladly add an additional `sequence_type` so that it supports all possible values, but I figured that would be a little overkill for this pull request. I hope that I have documented the class well enough that a would-be future developer wouldn't have a difficult time adding this behavior in the future if it is ever needed.

Let me know what you think and if I have adequately convinced you 😎 .  I'm always happy to discuss this code and the rationale behind the changes more.

No need to do that. I'm thinking that we can use directly the same computations that check for the intersection. This is related to the above comment that describes how to compute the distance from line to point.

1) Compute the distance from ray (line) to microphone (point)
2) If the distance is less than the receiver radius, there is an intersection
3) Use the point closest to the microphone on the ray to compute the distance. In fact, the traveled distance is already computed as a by-product of the intersection check.

I know that this makes the distance 'inexact', but if you think about it, the ratio of the error to the traveled distance is in fact quite small in most cases. This should be good enough for us. We need to keep in mind that the ray tracing process itself is an approximation of the true propagation of sound in space.

You're right the value of `SENTINEL` is never changed.

`SENTINEL` is used to tell client processes that their work is done. See https://github.com/certbot/certbot/pull/7057/files#diff-821c98fdcbcf065345fc89980bc6609eR343.

The way this works is the parent process loads the queue with a bunch of client instances that need tests run on them at https://github.com/certbot/certbot/pull/7057/files#diff-821c98fdcbcf065345fc89980bc6609eR542. The child processes work through this list until it reaches the `SENTINEL` value at which point they exit.

`SENTINEL` is a global constant for this purpose. For what it's worth, this aspect never changed. `SENTINEL` is currently a global constant that works that way in `master`, I just moved setting it's value to the top of the script so it is outside of the `main()` function.

The thing is that when we create NGram instance we don't yet have the schema (unless we load it manually from the dataset). This is inconvenient.

E.g.

```python
ngram=NGram(...)
with make_reader(url, schema_fields=ngram) as reader:
   ...
```
Then a user would have to do:
```python
schema=load_schema_from_dataset(url) # don't remember the exact function.
ngram=NGram(...)
with make_reader(url, schema_fields=ngram) as reader:
   ...
```

One way to address this is to defer resolution of regex to later, i.e.  

```python
class NGram(object):
...
   def resolve_regex(schema):
      ....
```

then in `make_reader`, once we have the schema at hand we call `ngram.resolve_regex(schema)`.

It's a bit counterintuitive at first, and it's extremely tempting if you're coming from e.g. a C#/Java standpoint where classes have reference semantics, but when you try it after a while, you see it doesn't actually buy you anything tangible in C++, and has an annoying habit of getting in your way over and over again. Conceptually, it's because it just doesn't really make sense in C++'s value semantics (unlike, say, Java's) to have some members be `const` and some not; it pretty much never makes sense to assign some members and not others.

If you want some practical reasons, some issues I can think of off the top of my head include the following (there are quite possibly more than I can't think of)

- It suppresses moves (because you can't move _from_ these members), which will make any code that attempts to copy the class inefficient for no good reason, since they now have to copy.

- It suppresses assignments, which makes it difficult to use the class in a container (since now you can't assign to each member in the container).

- If you really want to suppress moves and assignments, you should actually delete those members explicitly to document that fact, not do it implicitly like this.

- It triggers various compiler warnings (e.g. "assignment operator has been deleted") that could otherwise be occasionally helpful, but which now get incredibly noisy and which you'll want to turn off.

A lot of these reasons apply to references in a similar way as well, which is why I mentioned both together.

Technically, we need to decide on the scope of the dimension-symbols like M and N. The existing spec is
```
Currently, dimension variables are not scoped. A dimension variable "N" represents the same value
across the entire graph in a model. For example, if the graph has two inputs X and Y each with
shape ["N"], then at runtime the values passed in for X and Y MUST be tensors of rank 1 with the
same dimension. Nested sub-graphs currently share the same scope for dimension variables as the
main-graph. This allows a model to relate the dimensions of tensors inside the subgraph to the
dimensions of tensors in the outer graph.
```
(see under https://github.com/gramalingam/onnx/blob/master/docs/IR.md#static-tensor-shapes ). If we stick to this, we should visit all subgraphs and add their symbols right when constructing the top model-level symbol table.

The main argument I think against conflating `path` and `filename` is that if it becomes the same, it's a dual responsibility for different things. `path` is the absolute path in OS native file format (bytes or unicode) where the file is read from and `filename` is the relative filename what the file is reported at for the UI (unicode only).

So for instance a not uncommon situation is that you are trying to attach a logfile that has a filename such as /var/log/application-log-20201010.log` and you want to report it as `logfile.txt`.

>  And, out of curiosity, why does an envelope item has a slot for a filename instead of a full path?

Because that's what the file is reported at in the UI. We intentionally do not want to keep an entire file path there as it causes more complexities and does not solve a purpose for the user.

Thus they’re opaque to the caller, no?

Really we’re using *opaque* here as a shorthand for an existential type,
which in my experience is fairly standard: e.g., [Flow’s opaque
types][flow], and [Standard ML’s opaque vs. transparent
ascription][sml]. (They of course can’t be opaque to _every_ layer of
the stack: something has to introspect it, even if only as a byte
sequence. They’re opaque to the caller, which is what matters.)

If you want to omit the specific phrase “opaque key”, please specify
somewhere that clients must not inspect the content of the key in any
way.

[flow]: https://flow.org/en/docs/types/opaque-types/#opaque-type-alias-syntax-
[sml]: https://www.cs.cmu.edu/~rwh/introsml/modules/sigstruct.htm

graph.close()   # No more tasks can be added after this

I see the value in making it abundantly clear where to get the artifacts for the release. I just want to explain my position a little more and hear what you think. My small concern with splitting them is other than any deployment steps, I see no reason for the release pipeline and the advanced pipeline to ever diverge at all and running all tests again in a 2nd pipeline feels a little confusing and a waste of resources. Part of me wonders if it's simpler, especially in the long term, to keep everything in one pipeline and just have the final deployment steps conditional on some attribute like the presence of a git tag.

As for what action on GitHub triggers building the release, I've actually come full circle and think we should just build on tags and we won't push to `*.x` any earlier in the release process for now.

To tell you a little more about our release process at a high level so you can tell me what you think about that idea, currently before building the release, we make sure that all nightly tests have passed on the commit we want to release. This would include building the installer.

Once we've verified that, we make minor changes locally such as updating version numbers, dates, and signatures and then run some simple tests such as our unit tests on the built packages and a couple tests on certbot-auto to make sure everything still works. For example, here is the diffs made locally between the heavily tested commit and the release tag for our last release: https://github.com/certbot/certbot/compare/e3dbd9c...v0.39.0. After the minor additional testing has been done, we push the git tag, push the packages to PyPI, and then update `master` and the `*.x` branches in that order.

Now, in the 4 years of releasing Certbot I don't think we've ever had tests pass on the commit before we made changes and then fail on the tag. This and the fact that updating `*.x` earlier and waiting on the results further complicates the already painful release process (especially for point releases where `*.x` already exists as a protected branch), I'm think it's not necessary, we won't push `*.x` early, and we'll just build on the tag. One very nice thing about this is the history of the release pipeline is extremely clean. It's just our git tags.

Theoretically something could go wrong with building the installer, but I think the chances of it actually happening after it was built before our local changes and our tests have passed locally are extremely low and the complications that building it again earlier make to both the history of the release pipeline and the release process in general aren't worth it.

So there are multiple problems with this:
 - why are you trying to seed the dropout every time you do forward? It will not actually do anything I believe as `get_cudnn_dropout_desc` after the first call calls [cudnnRestoreDropoutDescriptor](https://docs.nvidia.com/deeplearning/sdk/cudnn-developer-guide/index.html#cudnnRestoreDropoutDescriptor) which seems to ignore the seed value. If you actually want to seed the dropout every time (via `cudnnSetDropoutDescriptor`) that would be super costly as seeding random number generator is way more expensive than actually using it.
 - you should never use `cudaMemcpy` in the operator code. If you really need to copy values from GPU to CPU you should use `cudaMemcpyAsync` followed by `cudaStreamSynchronize`. The difference is `cudaMemcpy` synchronizes on all streams (so it waits on all GPU activity and prevents all subsequent work to be done on all worker threads) vs `cudaStreamSynchronize` which waits only on the stream that you pass as argument (so other GPU workers are not affected).

The conflict here is that we're substituting the libjpeg library, which is used primarily by Pillow, however this may not affect us as the ABI between jpegturbo and libjpeg is the same AFAIK. 

Most Linux distributions distinguish between libturbojpeg (the library itself) and libjpeg-turbo (the libjpeg version using turbojpeg), thus, they do not have this problem, as we link against libturbojpeg, rather than against libjpeg-turbo.

In conda-forge, we have this conflict because the recipe for libjpeg-turbo produces both the turbo flavored libjpeg and libturbojpeg. I spoke to @isuruf, one of the maintainers in conda-forge, and he told me that they are working towards a solution in https://github.com/conda-forge/conda-forge.github.io/issues/673, but right now there are no alternative to this conflict. 

I guess we are not having any problem with this conflicting installation, as the tests are passing. However, we should encourage users to install PyTorch and torchvision, so we prevent other errors that could be caused as part of this conflict and we're not aware of

Great question and I've thought about this specific thing more than you might think! The problem with setting a default parameter to `[]` is that the parameter becomes that specific empty list object. If the code base did something to that list (which a good programmer never should) like append a value or something, it modifies the default "empty list" to now have an element in it. So in general it's considered bad practice to set a default argument to `[]`. In turn though, I found myself often forgetting to pass an empty list as that parameter all the time. So this "`if argument_list is None: ... argument_list = []` is a weird bastardization of "good" Python coding practice. I'm also in this headspace because we do this exact thing in the TaskGraph's `__init__` function, except there it makes more sense because we may modify those lists later.

if compression is None:

    try:

        # Arrow's compression inference on the file path doesn't work for Snappy, so

If a derived class implemented its own `destroy`, it would get called automatically instead (and was responsible for propagating upward via `super`).

I just tested again with the `hid_mcp2221` driver and the comment is indeed a bit misleading.  I checked that:
* sending the reset signal just after opening works without errors, and closes the device, so the `close()` after this point is not necessary:
  ```py
  >>> import hid; h = hid.device()
  >>> h.open(0x04D8, 0x00DD); report = b"\x70\xAB\xCD\xEF"; h.write(b"\0" + report + b"\0" * (64 - len(report)))
  65
  >>>
  ```
* trying to open the device again just after the reset signal fails with an `OSError` if there isn't enough delay, however the device is still considered closed, and if the `open()` is not successful the call to `.close()` is not necessary.

This `try`/`except` would still be useful is in case sending the reset signal fails, raising an error and leaving the device open, but I haven't encountered this situation.  Calling `.close()` on a closed device is a no-op though, so I initially added it just as an extra precaution.

Let me know if you still want me to revert the change or just update the comment to explain that the `self.close()` call is just there to guarantee that the device is indeed closed before re-raising.

I tried a bunch of different ways to implement this, but ran into problems with deciding which properties I wanted to prioritize. Here are the ones that I'm thinking about:

* define the rename-types with as little repetition as possible because they basically refer to the same idea

* ensure that it's quick to check if a node is in `noop_types` or `rename_types`. If, for instance, they weren't `frozenset`s but rather lists instead, there might be performance issues with manipulating very large schemas because we'd need to check many node types for each node in the schema AST.

* type-hint `_rename_name_and_add_to_record` so that `mypy` knows the necessary attributes exist (i.e. `node` has  `name` attribute)

* avoid needing to call `cast` by raising an assertion error if the node is the wrong type

The problem is that avoiding `cast` while being able to type-hint properly seems to require even more repetition of the same idea in code. I've tried several different ways to specify `<union types here>` so that we can write the assertion: passing in the `type_hint_rename_types` Union, passing in a set of the classes involved in `rename_types` (not the class names but the classes themselves), and passing in a tuple of the classes involved. This is the only thing that worked:

```
rename_types_classes = (
    EnumTypeDefinitionNode,
    InterfaceTypeDefinitionNode,
    NamedTypeNode,
    ObjectTypeDefinitionNode,
    UnionTypeDefinitionNode,
)
...
elif node_type in self.rename_types:
    # Rename node, put name pair into record
    if not isinstance(node, self.rename_types_classes):
        raise AssertionError
```

This didn't:
```
rename_types_classes = tuple(rename_types)
```

And this didn't either:
```
if not isinstance(node, tuple(self.rename_types)):
```

In other words, the only way I've found that satisfies `mypy` while avoiding `cast` involves making another representation of the same concept.

I could attempt to reduce this duplication by using Bojan's suggestion to dynamically compute `rename_types`, but that doesn't solve the problem of needing

* a `Union` for the type hints

* a tuple to avoid `cast()`

* a set to make membership checking quick

I asked about this possibility in the pip discord, and based on the answers there, my conclusion is that if we want to force a specific version of setuptools we have to keep the `--no-build-isolation` flag, but instead create a separated virtualenv and install the build deps there, to simulate pip's isolation feature.

---
An alternative suggestion was to try `PIP_CONSTRAINTS`.
To test this suggestion I locally tried:

- first build a wheel of the development version of setuptools via `python -m build --wheel`
  (`PIP_CONSTRAINTS` do not accept editable installs)
- Create a `constraints.txt` file with:
  ```
  setuptools @ file:///home/<...>/setuptools/dist/setuptools-58.5.3.post20211113-py3-none-any.whl
  ```
- Running:
1. `DISTUTILS_DEBUG=1 pip --verbose install -c constraints.txt dist/Brotli-1.0.9.zip`
2. `DISTUTILS_DEBUG=1 pip --verbose install -c constraints.txt --use-pep517 dist/Brotli-1.0.9.zip`
3. `DISTUTILS_DEBUG=1 PIP_CONSTRAINTS=./constraints.txt pip --verbose install --use-pep517 dist/Brotli-1.0.9.zip`

For (1) pip does not seem to respect the isolation very much, also there is nothing in the stdout/stderr that could be used to assert during the tests that the correct version of setuptools is being used.

For both (2) and (3), pip ignores the constraint and re-downloads `setuptools-58.5.3-py3-none-any.whl` from PyPI.

### requirements.txt

grpcio @ https://github.com/dagster-io/build-grpcio/raw/main/wheels/grpcio-1.47.2-cp39-cp39-macosx_11_0_arm64.whl

This does not validate that write_array has the correct dtype. If the array has the wrong dtype, this writes the wrong data.

LibraryInterpreter validates the array's dtype by specifying it on the ndpointer_wrapper.
Example code:
```
import nidaqmx
import numpy
from nidaqmx.constants import LineGrouping
from nidaqmx.stream_writers import DigitalSingleChannelWriter

with nidaqmx.Task() as task:
    task.do_channels.add_do_chan(
        "Dev1/port1/line0:3", line_grouping=LineGrouping.CHAN_FOR_ALL_LINES
    )

    writer = DigitalSingleChannelWriter(task.out_stream, auto_start=True)
    write_array = numpy.array([1, 2, 4, 8], dtype=numpy.uint8)
    print(writer.write_many_sample_port_byte(write_array))
```

With the correct dtype of uint8, it writes 4 bytes.

With the incorrect dtype of uint32, it raises an exception:
```
PS D:\dev\nidaqmx-python> poetry run python .\examples\do_sw_timed.py
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "D:\dev\nidaqmx-python\examples\do_sw_timed.py", line 14, in <module>
    print(writer.write_many_sample_port_byte(write_array))
  File "D:\dev\nidaqmx-python\generated\nidaqmx\stream_writers.py", line 836, in write_many_sample_port_byte
    return self._interpreter.write_digital_u8(
  File "D:\dev\nidaqmx-python\generated\nidaqmx\_library_interpreter.py", line 5850, in write_digital_u8
    error_code = cfunc(
ctypes.ArgumentError: argument 6: <class 'TypeError'>: array must have data type uint8
```

GrpcStubInterpreter is using `bytes(write_array)`. With a dtype of uint32, `print(bytes(write_array))` prints a lot of zero bytes:
```
b'\x01\x00\x00\x00\x02\x00\x00\x00\x04\x00\x00\x00\x08\x00\x00\x00'
```

Process ID: 0x0000246C         Thread ID: 0x00003F98
Start Time: 14:29:39.2682      Call Duration 00:00:00.0000
Status: 0 (0x0)

Yes all resumed jobs will have the same session id.

Yes if we run job A and B like you said, B will indeed delete cache.tar uploaded by job A (if it ends after job A) and upload its own one. But since cache stores the info to resume previous work files, 
- if B proceeds further than A, we should update the cache, which will have more info
- if B ends at the same stage as A or even ends earlier, what in A's cache.tar (which is downloaded to B's container) will still be included in B's cache.tar.

And since the cache.tar is always found using its path `project-id:nextflow_cache_db/<session id>/cache.tar` but not any job id, if the session is resumed again by job C, it would find this cache, and it should not matter if this tar is from A or B, because it always shall contains the most info of this session.

If we deleted the indexing logic in `sort_function`, the following would fail:
```python
import modin.pandas as pd; import numpy as np
df = pd.DataFrame(np.random.randint(0, 100, (1000, 100)), columns=[f"col {i}" for i in range(100)])
df['col 1'].value_counts()
```

It fails because the way our `value_counts` works is it masks, then does the `value_counts`, then does the sort, then finally renames the index. This means that during sorting, the name of the index and the name of the masked column are the same, and so pandas complains that we have an ambiguous label.

I first tried to work around it by reimplementing `Series.value_counts` to rename the index before doing the sort, but I discovered that there were other places where the Index was renamed but the rename was not propagated to the partitions, and so we would get the same ambiguous error label, so this takes care of the case when our index shares names with our columns.

I don't like this API design. Ideally, it shouldn't need any API changes. I believe the use case this PR suggests is:

1. The user serializes arrow data to byte[] or ByteBuffer.
2. The user invokes `Ray.putArrow(...)` to write serialized data to the object store with metadata "ARROW" and data as is.

I believe this is much better:

1. The user call `Ray.put(...)` with unserialized Arrow data objects.
2. Ray detects that the object to be serialized is an arrow data object, and invokes an arrow-specific method to serialize it to arrow IPC format represented by byte[] or ByteBuffer.
3. Ray writes serialized data to the object store with metadata "ARROW" and serialized data.

In theory, Ray Java should be able to support other IPC formats besides arrow. e.g. flatbuffer. I don't think adding new APIs is the right way to go.

One option is to move the processing into `HTMLRenderer.render_inline_code()`. The other renderers (LaTeX, Jira, XWiki20, AST, Scheme) will also need to be updated accordingly -- I don't know what the expected behavior is for them, but that should be possible to find out (and it's easy to just keep the current behavior). 1. Does `[{'method': 'x'}, {'method': 'y'}]` map to single call with two arguments (`obj.method('x', 'y')`) or to two individual calls with one argument (`obj.method('x'); obj.method('y')`)?

2. Instead of a list with one item dictionaries the format could be a list with internal lists (or tuples). Then `[('method', 'x'), ('method', 'y', 'z')]` could be interpreted as `obj.method('x'); obj.method('y', 'z')`. This ought to be pretty generic. It would handle even cases where arguments needs to be a list such as `[('method', [1, 2, 3])]` that would be `obj.method([1, 2, 3])`.

3. If the common case is setting attributes or calling methods only once and with one argument, then it might be a good idea to support specifying methods/attributes as a dictionary like `{'method': 'arg', 'attribute': 'value'}`. In the code this could be trivially converted to a list of lists (technically tuples) with code like `if isinstance(item, Mapping): item = item.items()`.

making this confidence score like this will indeed cause 1 extra query for each (test,environment) pair in the build. Given how large the tests table is, this will probably cause performance issues. What if you think of another design:

- call your `Confidence` class `FailureWithConfidence` instead
- have a single method in `Build`, called `failures_with_confidence`, that fetches all relevant `Test` objects in a single query, and then create the `FailureWithConfidence` objects interactively.

This might require you to change the design of `FailureWithConfidence` (prev. `Confidence`) slightly, like this:

```python
class FailureWithConfidence(object):
    def __init__(self, passes=0, count=0):
        self.passes = passes
        self.count = count

    def add_pass(self):
        self.passes += 1
        self.count += 1

    def add_failure(self):
        self.count += 1
```

(thread the above as pseudocode, it's untested)

The implementation of `Build.failures_with_confidence` could then look like the body of your view: iterate over the `Test` objects, collect the failure objects in a 2-dimensional array, creating new ones the first time it seems that (test name, environment) pair, and calling either `add_pass` or `add_failure`. You might create Failure objects for all tests, and the later discard the ones where `passes == count`.

To clarify, the `otLinkedBuffer`s in the proposed API don't correspond exactly to the packets being set out with TCP. For example, if each TCP packet is sized to include 462 bytes of data, each `otLinkedBuffer` in the send buffer could reference more than 462 bytes or less than 462 bytes.

To make TCP zero-copy in the long-term, we could modify OpenThread to allow IPv6 packets to be sent directly from `otLinkedBuffer` chains. Then the TCP stack could construct an `otLinkedBuffer` chain referencing data corresponding to segments in the send buffer to send them out. This could be done by allocating memory for new `otLinkedBuffer`s. Or, if we prefer to avoid allocating new `otLinkedBuffer`s, we could modify the `otLinkedBuffer` chain for the send buffer in-place to isolate individual segments to transmit them and then restore the original chain once transmission is complete.

- on_challenge is the method client libraries override to implement challenge handling for their particular service ([ARM, for example](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/blob/ddaf7529f8fb35c7888e5eeded1e0e0f956709cd/sdk/core/azure-mgmt-core/azure/mgmt/core/policies/_authentication.py#L51))

This is a very bad idea because make doesn't have any context into the Python world, nor is it aware of how to cache virtualenvs properly. It does not ensure the isolations between different envs at all being exposed to random and uncontrolled external influence. You could look into `nox`, if you want something configured via scripting but make is totally unfit for the task.

Also, many downstream packaging ecosystems have macros for working with tox-driven projects. They will (semi-)automatically pick up testing and building the docs. And they'll work with the PEP 517 setup seamlessly. Don't take this away from them.

3) This replaces the hard to make and read `[{...}, {...}]` format. 
4) Documentation will hopefully be less confusing, because there is only one format to explain.
